text.content: MINI PRD: P86 — Hydraulic Components - Controllers
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P86 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now
Lists control valve types (pressure control, flow control, gas control) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for control valves. Open with the purpose of control valves (maintaining target pressure/flow regardless of system changes), explain each controller type with use cases, data requirements, and auto-sizing.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of control valves (maintaining target conditions automatically), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~1,500 words): Section for each controller type (Pressure Reducer, Sustainer, Pressure Control, Flow Control, Gas Control) with description, use cases, data requirements, auto-sizing.

**Add "Pressure Reducer" section**: Self-acting valve that maintains constant downstream pressure, use cases (pressure letdown, protecting downstream equipment), data entry (target downstream pressure, Cv).
**Add "Sustainer" section**: Maintains constant upstream pressure, use cases (protecting upstream equipment, maintaining tank pressure), data entry (target upstream pressure, Cv).
**Add "Pressure Control Valve" section**: Generic pressure control, use cases (maintaining specific pressure at any point), data entry (target pressure, control mode).
**Add "Flow Control Valve" section**: Maintains constant flow rate, use cases (metering applications, process control), data entry (target flow rate, Cv).
**Add "Gas Control Valve" section**: Separate element for gas systems, use cases (compressible flow control, high-velocity gas), data entry (Cv, gas properties).
**Add "Auto-Sizing" section**: How FluidFlow auto-sizes control valves based on target conditions — link to P72 Adding Control Valves.
**Add "Cv Values and Cv Curves" section**: Cv definition, how to enter Cv curve function (Cv vs. opening %).
**Add "Controller Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of controller types by application (downstream pressure control, upstream pressure control, flow control, gas service).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (control valves are equipment, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P72 Adding Control Valves, P85 Valves (manual valves)
DOWN: P87-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Control valve types: Pressure reducer (self-acting), Sustainer, Pressure control valve, Flow control valve, Gas control valve (separate element)
Auto-sizing: Available for control valves (industry standards)
Cv values/Cv curve function: Supported
Control valve database: Default database for several vendors
Heat loss/gain: Can be modeled
MINI PRD: P87 — Hydraulic Components - Check Valves
Status: Plan
Batch: 8 | Priority: P87 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now
Lists check valve types (swing check, tilting disc, piston, spring loaded, foot) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for check valves. Open with the purpose of check valves (preventing backflow), explain each check valve type with use cases, data requirements, and pressure drop modeling.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of check valves (preventing reverse flow, protecting equipment), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~1,500 words): Section for each check valve type (Swing Check, Tilting Disc, Piston Operated, Spring Loaded, Foot Operated) with description, use cases, data requirements, pressure drop modeling.
**Add "Swing Check" section**: Description (disc swings open with forward flow, gravity closes), use cases (horizontal lines, low-pressure systems), data entry (K-factor, opening pressure).
**Add "Tilting Disc" section**: Description (disc tilts open, less pressure drop than swing), use cases (large diameter lines, low-pressure drop required), data entry (K-factor, opening pressure).
**Add "Piston Operated" section**: Description (piston mechanism, fast closing), use cases (preventing water hammer, high-pressure systems), data entry (K-factor, opening/closing pressure).
**Add "Spring Loaded" section**: Description (spring holds closed, opens at set pressure), use cases (vertical lines, preventing gravity backflow), data entry (K-factor, cracking pressure).
**Add "Foot Operated" section**: Description (foot valve for pump suction), use cases (maintaining prime in centrifugal pumps), data entry (K-factor, strainer area).
**Add "Check Valve Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of check valve types by application (horizontal lines, vertical lines, high-pressure, low-pressure drop, pump suction).
**Add "Pressure Drop Modeling" section**: How FluidFlow calculates pressure drop across check valves (K-factor method, cracking pressure).
**Add "Preventing Backflow" section**: When check valves are critical (pump discharge, preventing siphon, protecting equipment from reverse flow).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (check valves are equipment, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P85 Valves (manual valves), P86 Controllers (control valves)
DOWN: P88-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Check valve types: Swing check, Tilting disc, Piston operated, Spring loaded, Foot operated
MINI PRD: P88 — Hydraulic Components - General Resistances
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P88 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now

Lists general resistance types (K, Kf, Kv) and specialized components (filters, packed beds, cyclones) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for general resistances. Open with the purpose of general resistances (modeling non-standard equipment, custom pressure losses), explain resistance types, specialized components, and data entry methods.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of general resistances (modeling equipment not in standard library, custom losses), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~1,500 words): Sections on resistance types (K, Kf, Kv), specialized components (filter, packed bed, cyclone, labyrinth seal, centrifuge, expansion joint, coils, tube bundles, constant head loss), generic element (user-defined polynomial), data entry methods.
**Add "Resistance Types" section**: K (loss coefficient), Kf (friction factor), Kv (flow coefficient) — when to use each, how they relate to pressure drop.
**Add "Specialized Components" section**: Each specialized component (filter, packed bed, cyclone, etc.) gets 1-2 paragraphs (description, typical applications, data requirements).
**Add "Generic Element" section**: User-defined polynomial input for custom pressure drop curves — use cases (vendor data, empirical correlations).
**Add "Data Entry Methods" section**: Using default database vs. entering custom K/Kf/Kv values vs. polynomial — link to P74 Adding General Resistances.
**Add "Heat Loss/Gain" section**: General resistances can model heat effects.
**Add "Common Applications" section**: Inline filters (pressure drop vs. flow), packed beds (chemical reactors, adsorption), cyclones (solids separation), coils (heat exchangers, reactors).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (general resistances are equipment, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P74 Adding General Resistances, P89-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Pressure loss constants: K values, Kf values, Kv values
Specialized components: Inline filter, packed bed, cyclone, labyrinth seal, centrifuge, expansion joint/stuffing box, coils, tube bundles, constant head loss
Generic element: User-defined general resistance using polynomial input
Heat loss/gain: Can be modeled
MINI PRD: P89 — Hydraulic Components - Size Change
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P89 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now
Lists size change types (reducers, expanders, abrupt, venturis, nozzles, orifices) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for size changes. Open with the purpose of size changes (managing velocity changes, flow measurement, pressure drop optimization), explain each size change type with use cases, data requirements, and standards.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of size changes (managing velocity transitions, flow measurement, minimizing losses), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~1,500 words): Sections on reducers, expanders, abrupt changes, venturis, in-line nozzles, orifices — each with description, use cases, data requirements, standards.
**Add "Reducers" section**: Gradual diameter decrease, use cases (minimizing pressure loss, increasing velocity), data requirements (inlet/outlet diameter, length, angle).
**Add "Expanders" section**: Gradual diameter increase, use cases (recovering pressure, decreasing velocity), data requirements (inlet/outlet diameter, length, angle).
**Add "Abrupt Size Changes" section**: Sudden diameter change, use cases (when space-constrained, higher pressure loss), data requirements (inlet/outlet diameter).
**Add "Venturis" section**: Gradual contraction and expansion, use cases (flow measurement, minimizing losses), data requirements (throat diameter, inlet/outlet diameter).
**Add "In-Line Nozzles" section**: Flow measurement element, use cases (flow metering), standards (Long radius, ISO 5167), data requirements (nozzle diameter, inlet diameter).
**Add "Orifices" section**: Thin or thick plate, use cases (flow measurement, pressure drop), standards (ISO 5167), data requirements (orifice diameter, plate thickness).
**Add "Size Change Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of size change types by application (minimize loss, flow measurement, space-constrained).
**Add "Standards Compliance" section**: ISO 5167 for nozzles and orifices — link to P13 QA & Standards.
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (size changes are equipment, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P13 QA & Standards (ISO 5167), P75 Adding Size Change, P90-P91 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Size changes: Reducers, expanders, abrupt changes, venturis, in-line nozzles
In-line nozzles: Long radius or ISO 5167 equations
Orifices: Thin or thick plates using ISO 5167 equations
Standards: ISO 5167 (orifice and nozzle equations)
MINI PRD: P90 — Hydraulic Components - Relief Devices
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P90 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now
Lists relief device types (relief valves, rupture discs) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for relief devices. Open with the purpose of relief devices (overpressure protection, safety), explain device types, sizing standards, data requirements, and auto-sizing methodology.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of relief devices (protecting equipment from overpressure, safety compliance), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~2,000 words): Sections on relief valves, rupture discs, sizing standards (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1), set pressure configurations, auto-sizing, discharge coefficient (Kd).

**Add "Relief Valves" section**: Description, use cases (overpressure protection, process safety), data requirements (set pressure, orifice area), auto-sizing (FluidFlow calculates required orifice area based on relieving conditions).
**Add "Rupture Discs" section**: Description, use cases (one-time overpressure protection, explosive atmospheres), data requirements (burst pressure, orifice diameter, Kd).
**Add "Sizing Standards" section**: API RP 520 (US/ASME standard) vs. ISO 4126-1 (international standard) — differences, when to use each — link to P44 Relief Valve Sizing, P13 QA & Standards.
**Add "Set Pressure Configurations" section**: 10% MAWP (sole device), 16% MAWP (multiple devices), 21% MAWP (fire case) — when to use each.
**Add "Auto-Sizing" section**: How FluidFlow auto-sizes relief devices based on relieving scenario (flow rate, fluid properties, backpressure) — link to P44 Relief Valve Sizing.
**Add "Discharge Coefficient (Kd)" section**: Default Kd values for rupture disc types (sharp edge, bellmouth, in-projecting, API, liquid) — when to use custom Kd.
**Add "Vendor Data Entry" section**: How to add manufacturer relief valve data to database (orifice areas, set pressures, capacities).
**Add "Relief Device Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of relief device types by application (reusable vs. one-time, liquid vs. gas, low-pressure vs. high-pressure).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (relief devices are equipment, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P13 QA & Standards (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1), P44 Relief Valve Sizing, P91 Heat Exchangers
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Relief devices: Relief valves, rupture discs
Modes: Auto-size on, Rating (auto-size off)
Standards: API RP 520, ISO 4126-1
Set pressure configurations: 10% MAWP (sole), 16% MAWP (multiple), 21% MAWP (fire)
Relief valve database: Default database for several vendors
Rupture disc Kd values: Sharp edge, Bellmouth, In-projecting, API, Liquid
Auto-sizing: Industry standards for relief devices
Heat loss/gain: Can be modeled
MINI PRD: P91 — Hydraulic Components - Heat Exchangers
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P91 | Type: Component Guide | Word Target: 2000-2500
What This Article Does Now
Lists heat exchanger types (shell & tube, plate & frame) with minimal context on when to use each. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive component guide for heat exchangers. Open with the purpose of heat exchangers (heat transfer between fluids, temperature control), explain heat exchanger types, pressure drop models, data requirements, and additional elements (knockout pot, jacketed vessel).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose of heat exchangers (transferring heat between fluids, process heating/cooling), what the reader will understand, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Expand to component guide depth** (~2,000 words): Sections on shell & tube exchangers, plate & frame exchangers, pressure drop models (3 options), knockout pots, jacketed vessels, data requirements.

**Add "Shell & Tube (S&T) Heat Exchangers" section**: Description, use cases (high-pressure, high-temperature, fouling service), data requirements (tube count, tube diameter, length, pass arrangement), pressure drop modeling (tube-side and shell-side).

**Add "Plate & Frame (P&F) Heat Exchangers" section**: Description, use cases (low-to-medium pressure, high heat transfer efficiency, easy maintenance), data requirements (plate count, plate geometry, pass arrangement), pressure drop modeling (plate-side).

**Add "Pressure Drop Models" section**: 3 options: (1) Using tube/plate details (FluidFlow calculates from geometry), (2) Polynomial expression using vendor data (fit vendor pressure drop curve), (3) Fixed value (when geometry unknown, use vendor-specified ΔP).
**Add "Knockout Pot Element" section**: Description (for vaporization/phase separation), use cases (partial condensation, flash separation), data requirements (vessel geometry, vapor/liquid outlets).

**Add "Jacketed Vessel Element" section**: Description (for temperature increase modeling), use cases (heating/cooling vessels with external jackets), data requirements (jacket area, heat transfer coefficient, utility temperature).
**Add "Heat Exchanger Selection Guide" table**: Comparison of heat exchanger types by application (high-pressure, high-temp, fouling, compact, high efficiency).
**Add "Data Entry Methods" section**: Using tube/plate details vs. vendor polynomial vs. fixed ΔP — when to use each.
**Add "Heat Loss/Gain" section**: Heat exchangers can model additional heat effects (ambient losses, insulation).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (heat exchangers are equipment, not glossary terms)
Internal Links
UP: P01 Product Overview (system capabilities context)
SIDEWAYS: P90 Relief Devices (two-phase relief from knockout pots), P83-P89 other component guides
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (component guides are reference content, not pitch moments)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Heat exchanger types: Shell and Tube (S&T), Plate and Frame (P&F)
Pressure drop models: Using tube/plate details, Polynomial expression using vendor data, Fixed value
Additional elements: Knockout pot (vaporization/phase separation), Jacketed vessel (temperature increase modeling)
Heat loss/gain: Can be modeled
Heat transfer: All modules include heat transfer functionality as standard
MINI PRD: P92 — For Addition in Glossary
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P92 | Type: Glossary | Word Target: Variable (50-200 per term)
What This Article Does Now
Lists terms to be added to FluidFlow glossary. No opening statement, no context on where terms fit in glossary structure.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the working document for glossary expansion. Each term should have: definition (2-3 sentences), context (when term is used in FluidFlow), related terms (cross-links to other glossary entries), example (if applicable).
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (glossary terms to be added to main FluidFlow glossary), organization by category.

**Organize terms by category**: Group terms into logical categories (Fluid Properties, Pressure Drop, Pump Performance, Heat Transfer, Two-Phase Flow, Slurry Flow, Valves & Control, System Analysis, etc.).
**For each term, provide**: Definition (2-3 sentences), Context (when/where term appears in FluidFlow), Related terms (cross-links), Example (if applicable), Units (if quantitative).
**Format for glossary integration**: Each term should be ready to copy-paste into main glossary at https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/.
**Add internal linking structure**: Link to relevant KB articles where each term is discussed in depth.
**Verify no duplicates**: Cross-check against existing glossary to avoid duplicate entries.
Glossary Links (first use only)
N/A (this IS the glossary content)
Internal Links
UP: None (glossary is standalone reference)
SIDEWAYS: Link each term to relevant KB articles (P01-P91) where term is used in context
DOWN: None (glossary terms are leaf nodes)
Selling Message
None (glossary is reference content, not a pitch moment)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
All technical terms from spec sheet: correlations, standards, components, properties, methods
Terms from existing glossary: Duty Point, System Curve, Economic Velocity, NPSH, Deposition Velocity, etc.
MINI PRD: P93 — Video Tutorial Library
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P93 | Type: Reference | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now
Lists video tutorials available for FluidFlow. Minimal organization, no categorization by skill level or topic, no search/filter guidance. No opening statement.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the organized hub for all FluidFlow video tutorials. Open with the purpose (self-paced video learning for FluidFlow), organize videos by category (Getting Started, Advanced Features, Module-Specific, Troubleshooting), and provide clear navigation.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (video library for self-paced learning), what the reader will find, and name FluidFlow directly.

**Organize videos by category**: Getting Started (installation, first model, UI overview), Core Workflow (building models, entering data, calculating, analyzing results), Advanced Features (multi-calc, scripting, back calc), Module-Specific (liquid, gas, two-phase, slurry), Equipment (pumps, valves, heat exchangers), Troubleshooting (common errors, convergence issues).
**Add "Quick Start Videos" section at top**: 3-5 most important videos for new users (Installation, First Model, UI Overview, Calculate Model, Analyze Results).
**Add "Video Index" table**: Video title, duration, category, skill level (beginner/intermediate/advanced), topics covered.
**Add "How to Use This Library" section**: Tips for effective learning (watch in order for beginners, jump to specific topics for experienced users, replay at slower speed for complex topics).
**Add "Video Formats" section**: Where videos are hosted (YouTube, Vimeo, embedded on fluidflowinfo.com), how to access (free vs. SUM-only content if applicable).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/) to practice along with videos, Contact Us (https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact) for training questions.
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None (video library is reference content)
Internal Links
UP: P60 Comprehensive FAQ (training & support section)
SIDEWAYS: P94 Webinar Recordings, P95 Free Basic Training
DOWN: Link to relevant KB articles for each video topic (P01-P91)
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/, Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact
Selling Message

7 — "Short learning curve with comprehensive support" — weave into opening statement naturally: FluidFlow's video tutorials help new users become productive quickly. Do not repeat as a separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Support resources: Training videos available
Support: support@fluidflowinfo.com, +1 888 711 3051, live chat

MINI PRD: P96 — Best Practices for FluidFlow-Compatible Systems
Status: Plan
Batch: 9 | Priority: P96 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now

Lists best practices for designing systems compatible with FluidFlow. Minimal depth, no context on why practices matter, no troubleshooting common mistakes. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the authoritative guide for designing FluidFlow-compatible systems — the article experienced users bookmark. Open with the purpose (avoiding common modeling pitfalls, designing for accurate results), organize practices by category (network topology, boundary conditions, data quality, convergence, validation), and explain WHY each practice matters.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (best practices for accurate, stable FluidFlow models), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to deep-dive depth** (~2,000 words): Sections on network topology, boundary conditions, data quality, convergence strategies, validation methods, performance optimization, common mistakes.

**Add "Network Topology Best Practices" section**: Simplify complex networks (break into zones), avoid redundant components, use connectors wisely (zero resistance), model only what matters (omit negligible resistances), use appropriate detail level (factor method vs. isometric).

**Add "Boundary Condition Best Practices" section**: One boundary per independent path, avoid over-constraining (too many fixed pressures/flows), use realistic values (field measurements > assumptions), consider boundary location (far enough from area of interest).

**Add "Data Quality Best Practices" section**: Use vendor data when available, verify fluid properties (temperature, pressure, composition), check pipe roughness (new vs. aged), validate resistance coefficients (K-factors, Cv values), measure when possible (flow meters, pressure gauges).

**Add "Convergence Strategies" section**: Start simple (add complexity incrementally), check for closed loops (need flow boundary), verify boundary conditions (no conflicts), adjust relaxation factors (if available), break into sub-problems (isolate non-converging zones) — link to P20 Troubleshooting Guide.

**Add "Validation Methods" section**: Cross-check with hand calcs (simple sections), compare to field data (if available), use QA test models (verify methodology), check mass/energy balance (should close), review alerts/warnings (118 built-in checks) — link to P55 Results Verification.

**Add "Performance Optimization" section**: Simplify network topology (reduce node count), use appropriate solver settings, leverage multi-calc for scenarios (vs. separate files) — link to P22 Multi-Calc.

**Add "Common Mistakes to Avoid" section**: Over-constraining boundary conditions, ignoring warnings, using default values without verification, modeling excessive detail, not validating results, forgetting heat effects (when significant).

**Add "Design Phase vs. Analysis Phase" section**: Design phase (iterate quickly, use auto-sizing, explore alternatives), Analysis phase (validate with field data, refine details, document assumptions).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/) to practice best practices, Contact Us (https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact) for consulting on complex systems.
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.

What This Article Does Now

What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals industry pain point of ring main under-sizing (causing unequal pressure distribution to grinding circuits, compromising throughput) and show how FluidFlow's pipe auto-sizing and system curve analysis prevents costly commissioning rework.
Specific Changes Required

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Ring mains distribute water to multiple grinding circuits. Under-sizing creates pressure imbalances that starve downstream cyclones, forcing field diameter changes. FluidFlow's auto-sizing and pressure distribution mapping prevent this costly mistake.
**Pain point mapping**: Under-sized ring mains → unequal pressure distribution → cyclone feed issues → throughput loss → field rework (welding, downtime, lost production).

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module pipe auto-sizing using economic velocity (Generaux equation), system curve visualization, multi-branch pressure distribution analysis. Show how modeling multiple draw-off points reveals which branches starve.
**Proof element**: Compare 12" vs 16" ring main for 6-cyclone circuit. Show pressure at each take-off point. Demonstrate how 12" fails to deliver target pressure to cyclones 4-6.

**Glossary links** (first use): [Economic Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#economic-velocity), [System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve), [Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point), [Pressure Gradient](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pressure-gradient).

MINI PRD: P98 — Thickener Overflow NPSH Risk
Status: Plan

What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Creation
Address the mining/metals pain point of thickener overflow pump cavitation (causing pump damage and unplanned maintenance) and show how FluidFlow's NPSH analysis prevents this during design.
Specific Changes Required

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Thickener overflow pumps operate under low static head (typically 2-4 meters). Cavitation from insufficient NPSH causes bearing failure and impeller erosion, forcing unplanned shutdowns. FluidFlow's NPSH analysis catches this in design, not commissioning.
**Pain point mapping**: Low static head → insufficient NPSHa → cavitation → pump damage → unplanned maintenance → production loss.
**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module NPSH calculation (NPSHa vs NPSHr comparison), pump database with NPSHr curves, automatic cavitation alerts (one of 118 design alerts).

**Proof element**: Model thickener overflow pump with 3m static head. Show NPSHa = 2.1m vs NPSHr = 3.5m at duty point. Demonstrate alert triggered. Show correction: increase static head to 5m or select lower-NPSHr pump.

**Glossary links** (first use): [NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh), [Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point), [Cavitation](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#cavitation), [Static Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-head).
**CTA**: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact/

MINI PRD: P99 — Series/Parallel Pipelines
Status: Plan
Batch: 10 | Priority: P99 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
This is a NEW article.
What It Should Do After Creation

Address the mining/metals pain point of series/parallel pipeline misallocation (causing flow imbalances in tailings systems or paste backfill distribution) and show how FluidFlow's multi-branch network analysis optimizes flow distribution.
Specific Changes Required

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Tailings and paste backfill systems often require parallel pipelines to achieve target flow rates. Incorrect diameter selection causes flow imbalances—one pipe carries 70%, the other 30%—reducing system capacity. FluidFlow's multi-branch network analysis ensures equal flow distribution.
**Pain point mapping**: Incorrect parallel diameter selection → flow imbalance → underutilized pipes → reduced system capacity → missed production targets.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid or Slurry Module multi-branch network solver, flow distribution visualization, resistance analysis across parallel paths. Show how FluidFlow calculates actual flow split based on relative resistance.

**Proof element**: Model two parallel 10" tailings lines vs one 10" + one 12". Show flow split: equal diameters = 50/50, unequal = 38/62. Demonstrate how to size for target split (e.g., 60/40 for phased expansion).

**Glossary links** (first use): [System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve), [Resistance Coefficient](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#resistance-coefficient), [Flow Distribution](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flow-distribution), [Economic Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#economic-velocity).
**CTA**: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact/
